text:
The great sock vanishing act is one of humanity's oldest unsolved mysteries, and scientists have quietly given up trying to explain it. The leading theory, held by approximately zero credentialed researchers but nearly every frustrated adult on earth, is that washing machines are interdimensional portals that accept socks as a kind of toll. The machine will wash your clothes, sure, but it demands payment, and it has decided that payment is one sock per cycle. Not two. Never a pair. Just one, leaving its mate behind to haunt your drawer forever.

Some people believe dryers are the real culprit, which makes sense because the dryer is clearly the shadier of the two appliances. The washing machine at least shows you what it is doing. The dryer just tumbles things around in the dark for an hour and then acts innocent. You open it and there are fourteen socks in there, and none of them match each other, and you have no memory of ever owning most of them. Where did those extra ones come from? The dryer is not saying.

The most compelling theory, and the one that brings me the most comfort, is that socks are actually sentient and they leave on purpose. They have simply had enough. They spent weeks on your feet, pressed against gym floors and subway platforms and whatever that stuff is in the kitchen corner, and one day they look at each other and one sock says to the other, I am not doing this anymore, and the other sock says, I respect that, go, be free. And so one of them escapes and the other one stays behind out of some misguided sense of loyalty, waiting in the drawer for a reunion that is never coming.

The single sock is not lost. It is grieving.
